items.q: What happens in Stage 2 of the Demographic Transition Model?
items.correct: Death rates fall rapidly (due to better food, sanitation and medicine) while birth rates remain high, causing rapid population growth.
items.misconception: Birth rates rise rapidly because people want larger families.
items.random_wrong: Both birth and death rates fall sharply in Stage 2.
items.why: The population explosion in Stage 2 is driven by falling deaths, not rising births. Students see growth and assume more babies are being born when in fact more babies are surviving.

items.q: Why can a cold winter NOT disprove climate change?
items.correct: Climate is the long-term statistical average; a single cold winter is a weather event. Climate change is the trend in averages and extremes over decades, not the absence of cold days.
items.misconception: A really cold winter proves global warming is not happening.
items.random_wrong: Climate change only happens in summer.
items.why: This is one of the most common public misconceptions. Climate is the slope of the trend over 30+ years; individual weather events are scatter around that line — both can be cold.

items.q: What are the four pillars of food security?
items.correct: Availability (enough food exists), access (people can obtain it), utilisation (nutritional quality and absorption) and stability (consistent over time) — defined by the FAO.
items.misconception: Food security just means producing enough food.
items.random_wrong: Food security means storing food for emergencies.
items.why: Most modern famines occur where food is available but inaccessible (Amartya Sen's entitlement theory). Production alone doesn't deliver security — distribution, affordability and nutrition all matter.

items.correct: Chinese claim over much of the South China Sea
items.misconception: Maritime boundary between Russia and Japan
items.why: Correct answer: Chinese claim over much of the South China Sea. “Maritime boundary between Russia and Japan” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which body coordinates Arctic governance among 8 states?
items.why: It's Arctic Council. “NATO” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many member states are in the Arctic Council?
items.why: The real figure is 8. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
